title: What is an array
instruction: An array is a shelf with a fixed number of slots, all the same type. Run this to see five slots all starting at zero.
successMessage: Five slots, all zero. Go fills every slot with the zero value of the type.

title: Declare with values
instruction: Fill a shelf right when you create it: `books := [3]string{"Dune", "Neuromancer", "Snow Crash"}`. Change the titles to your own favorites.
hint: Replace the three book titles inside the curly braces with your own. Keep the size [3].

codeChecks.message: Keep the [3]string format and provide three string values.
Change the book titles to your own choices.

successMessage: Size in brackets. Type after. Values inside curly braces.

title: Access by index
instruction: Shelf slots start at 0, not 1. `books[0]` is the first, `books[2]` is the third. Print each title on its own line using its index.

codeChecks.message: Access the first book with books[0]
Access the second book with books[1]
Access the third book with books[2]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Indexes start at 0. Always. In every programming language.

title: Update a value
instruction: Change any slot with `shelf[index] = value`. Change slot [1] from 65 to 88 and run it to see the before and after.
hint: shelf[1] = 88 — add this line between the two Println calls.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: Read with brackets. Write with brackets and equals. The slot changes, everything else stays.

title: Loop through an array
instruction: Use a `for` loop with `len()` to print every element automatically. Add the loop to print all five shelf values.
hint: for i := 0; i < len(shelf); i++ { fmt.Println(shelf[i]) }

codeChecks.message: Start your loop at i := 0 since arrays are zero-indexed.
Use len(shelf) as the loop condition.
Access each element using shelf[i] inside the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: One loop, any size. This pattern — loop from 0 to len — is one of the most common things in Go.

title: range — the cleaner loop
instruction: `for i, v := range shelf` gives you index and value. No manual counter, no `len()`. Change this loop to use `range`.
hint: for i, v := range books { fmt.Println(i, v) } — replace the entire for loop.

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for i, v := range books
Replace the old loop with a range loop.

successMessage: range gives you the index and value in one shot. Cleaner, more Go-like.

title: Arrays are fixed size 🤯
instruction: This program tries to put 5 books in a 3-slot shelf. Run it, read the error, then fix it by changing `[3]` to `[5]`.
hint: Change [3]string to [5]string to fit all five book titles.
expectedOutput: [Dune Neuromancer Snow Crash Hyperion Altered Carbon]

codeChecks.message: Change [3]string to [5]string to fit all five values.

successMessage: You cannot stretch an array. That rigidness is a feature — it guarantees the size is always known. For flexible lists, that's what slices are for.

title: Find the thickest book
instruction: Loop through page counts and track the largest. Start with `pages[0]` as the thickest, then update if you find a bigger one.
hint: Start with thickest := pages[0]. Loop from index 1. If pages[i] > thickest, update it. Print thickest after the loop.

codeChecks.message: Start with thickest := pages[0] as your initial best guess.
Compare each element: if pages[i] > thickest, update it.
Print thickest after the loop ends.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Scanning for a max value powers search engines, leaderboards, and analytics.
